ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class; no specific entry for Teh O (sweetened black tea without milk). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, infusions and other hot beverages
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tea and hot infusion beverages broadly; no specific code for sweetened black tea without milk (Teh O). Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to tea as a substance; no specific concept for Teh O with sugar and no milk. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Teh O served at a Singapore kopitiam or café, approximately 250 ml, containing brewed black tea sweetened with white sugar and no milk.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355); sugar contribution estimated from standard kopitiam preparation (approximately 2 teaspoons / 8 g sugar per 250 ml serving)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea values taken from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171917). Sugar content adapted to reflect standard Singapore kopitiam Teh O preparation: approximately 8 g sugar per 250 ml serving, yielding ~3.2 g sugar per 100 ml. Energy recalculated accordingly. Protein and fat remain effectively zero as in plain black tea. Sodium from USDA source. This is a per-100 ml representation of the prepared beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; plain tea and black tea entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain black tea has a negligible glycemic index as it contains no glycemic carbohydrates in the tea itself. The added sugar (sucrose) in Teh O contributes carbohydrate, but at the diluted concentration present in a standard serving (~3.2 g per 100 ml), the overall beverage GI is effectively negligible. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving = (1 × 8.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.08, rounded to 0.0. Source: Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care; University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Teh O is suitable for individuals with hypertension as its sodium content is negligible (~7.5 mg per 250 ml serving); however, patients should monitor total daily sugar intake from sweetened beverages as part of overall dietary management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (sucrose) raises postprandial blood glucose; approximately 8 g sugar per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid Teh O with sugar, as the approximately 8 g of added sucrose per 250 ml serving contributes to postprandial glycaemic excursion; requesting Teh O Kosong (unsweetened) is the preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol; black tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Teh O is low in saturated fat and cholesterol and is generally acceptable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; the polyphenol content of black tea has been associated with modest improvements in endothelial function in peer-reviewed literature, though the sugar content should be minimised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh O is a classic Singaporean kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) beverage consisting of brewed black tea sweetened with white sugar and served without milk. The name derives from Hokkien: 'teh' means tea and 'O' means black or plain (without milk). It is one of the most ubiquitous drinks in Singapore's hawker centres and kopitiams, typically prepared by pulling strong Ceylon or local-blend black tea through a muslin sock filter, then sweetening with white sugar to taste. The beverage has a deep amber colour, a robust astringent flavour from the tannins in black tea, and a clean sweet finish. It can be served hot (Teh O Panas) or iced (Teh O Ais). Nutritionally, Teh O is low in calories (~32 kcal per 250 ml serving), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives its carbohydrate content entirely from added sugar (~8 g per serving). It is dairy-free, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals. The sodium content is very low. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the added sugar is a concern and the unsweetened version (Teh O Kosong) is recommended. Black tea contains polyphenols including theaflavins and thearubigins, which have been associated with antioxidant and modest cardioprotective effects in epidemiological studies.
common_user_queries: Is Teh O the same as black tea?
How many calories in Teh O?

What is the difference between Teh O and Teh?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central plain black tea data (FDC ID 171917) with sugar content estimated from standard Singapore kopitiam preparation practice. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Teh O with sugar was available at time of processing. The adaptation is well-supported and defensible but is not an exact match from a primary regional source.
notes: 1. Per_100g values represent per 100 ml of the prepared beverage (density approximated as 1 g/ml for dilute aqueous beverage). 2. fat_g is naturally zero as plain brewed black tea contains no fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are naturally zero as this is a fat-free aqueous beverage. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as brewed tea contains no dietary fibre in the liquid phase. 5. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no milk. 6. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 7. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 8. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 9. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 11. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 12. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 13. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 14. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 15. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for brewed black tea. 16. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates from tea itself; the added sugar is diluted; gi_category low per convention for beverages with negligible GI impact. 17. Input cuisine tag 'Italian' is incorrect; food is a Singaporean kopitiam beverage and has been reclassified accordingly. 18. protein_g is 0.1 g per 100 ml, a trace amount from brewed black tea; this is not zero and requires no zero justification.
